data.title: Intel in India for the "Long Haul," Intel Stock (NASDAQ:INTC) Still Slips
data.description:
Chip stock Intel (INTC) is ready to support India, and means to be in for the long haul, reports note. Intel has a plan to support Indian operations, including not only semiconductor manufacturing but also artificial intelligence (AI) development as well. Investors were somewhat dissatisfied with this notion, and sent shares down fractionally in Friday afternoon's trading.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

CNBC recently discussed the matter with Santhosh Viswanathan, managing director of Intel India. And Viswanathan had no shortage of praise to offer Intel for its support of Intel India. While certainly, Intel India is poised to drive Intel's operations in producing semiconductors, the connection to Intel India will go beyond that as well.

As for Intel's plans in India, Viswanathan made it clear that Intel was in for the "long haul." Intel also has a hand in supporting the overall technological ecosystem in India, from manufacture to telecom and beyond. A connection to Tata is also helping matters here for Intel India, as it works to produce AI PCs. Remember just a few days ago when Intel declared that one in two PCs would be AI PCs? The work Intel is currently doing in India would readily support this idea.

A Comparative Deal

For anyone who is looking for a new Intel processor, though, things might seem a bit disheartening. The ongoing shortage around processors, and their steadily-surging price tags, is not helping matters. But new reports say that shopping Amazon (AMZN) will prove helpful for anyone who wants to get an Intel Core Ultra 9 285K.

That processor is currently down to $559.77, which is a decent discount off its standard manufacturer's suggested retail price (MSRP) of $599. It is hardly a fire sale—and given the circumstances that would be expecting too much anyway—but it will be a welcome discount for anyone needing a new processor right now.

Is Intel a Buy, Hold or Sell?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Hold consensus rating on INTC stock based on eight Buys, 21 Holds and four Sells assigned in the past three months, as indicated by the graphic below. After a 79.41% rally in its share price over the past year, the average INTC price target of $48.21 per share implies 9.08% upside potential.
data.author: Steve Anderson

data.title: Anthropic Introduces New Cybersecurity Feature to Claude Code Platform
data.description:
Anthropic has introduced a new cybersecurity feature called Claude Code Security, which is built directly into its Claude Code platform on the web. Interestingly, this caused cybersecurity stocks to slide, with CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Cloudflare (NET) each falling roughly 6% at the time of writing. For now, the new tool is being released as a limited research preview for Enterprise and Team customers. Anthropic says the goal is to gather real-world feedback so the technology can be improved and deployed safely.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In addition, maintainers of open-source projects are being offered fast-track access at no cost, though they must apply to participate. The company hopes that this collaborative approach will help refine the system. Importantly, Claude Code Security works by scanning software code for vulnerabilities and then recommending specific fixes that human developers can review. This process is designed to find weaknesses that traditional security tools may overlook.

However, Anthropic also admitted that the same AI capabilities that help defenders identify flaws could potentially help attackers exploit them. Therefore, the company says that its intention is to put this powerful technology in the hands of defenders first. In addition, Anthropic said that the feature builds on more than a year of research into Claude's cybersecurity abilities.

Which Cybersecurity Stock Is the Better Buy?

Turning to Wall Street, out of the two stocks mentioned above, analysts think that CRWD stock has more room to run than NET. In fact, CRWD's price target of $549.43 per share implies 39.5% upside versus NET's 28.2%.
data.author: Vince Condarcuri

data.title: Trump Rolls Out '10% Global Tariff' with Section 301 Investigations Underway
data.description:
On Friday, the Supreme Court ruled to strike down President Trump's tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). However, Trump is moving quickly to implement tariffs using other legal measures.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

"Today I will sign an Order to impose a 10% GLOBAL TARIFF, under Section 122, over and above our normal TARIFFS already being charged," Trump said in a Truth Social post.

Trump Turns to Section 122 and Section 301 to Impose Tariffs

Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 allows the president to impose tariffs of up to 15% for a maximum of 150 days, extendable with congressional approval. The statute can be used to address trade deficits, does not require a federal investigation, and has never been invoked by a president until now.

Trump added that he would begin the process of Section 301 investigations. Section 301, under the same trade act as Section 122, authorizes the U.S. Trade Representative to investigate unfair trade practices and the president to impose tariffs or other trade penalties based on those findings.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Dow Jones Today: DJIA Edges Higher as SCOTUS Strikes Down Trump's Tariffs; GDP Falls Short
data.description:
The Dow Jones (DJIA) is trading slightly higher on Friday after the Supreme Court issued a ruling on the legality of President Trump's tariffs alongside a GDP update from the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA).

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In a 6-3 ruling, SCOTUS ruled that Trump's tariffs are illegal and said he overstepped his authority when using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to enforce them. The act allows the president to impose economic sanctions, such as tariffs, on foreign nations to address national emergencies.

"The president asserts the extraordinary power to unilaterally impose tariffs of unlimited amount, duration, and scope," wrote Chief Justice John Roberts. "In light of the breadth, history, and constitutional context of that asserted authority, he must identify clear congressional authorization to exercise it."

However, SCOTUS didn't provide guidance on a tariff refund process and warned that it "is likely to be a 'mess,' as was acknowledged at oral arguments." The U.S. could be on the hook for $175 billion of refunds, according to Penn-Wharton Budget Model economists.

Trump labeled the ruling as a "disgrace" and reiterated that he has a backup plan. The plan could include Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, which allows tariffs of up to 15% for a maximum of 150 days. Trump may also turn to Section 338 of the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, which permits tariffs of up to 50% on countries identified as engaging in unfair trade practices with the U.S., according to Capital Economics Chief North America economist Paul Ashworth.

Meanwhile, the BEA announced this morning that U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) grew at an annualized rate of 1.4% during the fourth quarter, below the estimate of 3% and down from 4.4% in the prior quarter. Consumer spending and investment boosted GDP, while the effects of the government shutdown and declining exports weighed it down.

The Dow Jones is up by 0.07% at the time of writing.
data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: 'Fighting the AI Ghost Trade' – Dan Ives Lists 10 Catalysts to Reverse the Tech Selloff
data.description:
Wedbush's top analyst Daniel Ives called the current tech sector selloff the "AI Ghost Trade" and listed 10 catalysts that could help reverse course. These 10 potential developments could help reenergize belief in the tech sector and dispel fears of AI overtaking software companies. Ives is a five-star analyst on TipRanks, ranking #636 out of 12,108 analysts tracked. He has a 53% success rate and an average return per rating of 13.4%.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Ives, with 25 years' tracking the tech sector, dismissed market fears of overhyped trends killing industries. He pointed to Microsoft's (MSFT) mid-2000s cybersecurity push, which skeptics claimed would wipe out the sector. Yet the RSA Conference in San Francisco grew from 3,000 to 40,000 attendees. He also recalled predictions a decade ago that Intel (INTC) would rule the chip industry, while Nvidia (NVDA) chips were seen as ideal only for gaming, before Nvidia powered the AI boom.

He compared today's market panic to past overblown fears that never panned out. Even with hyperscalers poised to spend nearly $700 billion in AI capex this year, investors worry startups will topple big tech giants.

Ives' Top 10 Catalysts to Reverse the Tech AI trade    

OpenAI – The ChatGPT maker completes its $100 billion funding round, putting rumors to rest.

Nvidia – CEO Jensen Huang reiterates massive AI chip demand on next week's Q4 earnings call, while surpassing expectations.

Oracle (ORCL) – Oracle sees early success with its $45 billion to $50 billion capital raise plans.

Salesforce (CRM) – CRM beats Q4 results next week and shows strong AI monetization in earnings and guidance.

M&A – Software sector mergers and acquisitions ramp up over the next few months, involving large public companies.

Apple (AAPL) – Apple finally rolls out the first phase of its long-awaited Siri AI updates, setting the consumer AI story in motion.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) – CrowdStrike showcases AI monetization in its upcoming Q4 results, becoming the first cloud security player in the AI space.

MSFT, NOW – Microsoft and ServiceNow (NOW) start displaying AI monetization in their March-quarter results.

META, GOOGL – Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) ramp up AI-driven digital advertising monetization in the first half of 2026.

Anthropic – Enterprises start using Anthropic's Claude models but face scaling and security issues, easing fears that these models will replace software firms.

Which Is the Best AI Stock from Ives' List?

We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool to see which stocks from Ives' list are currently favored by analysts.

Wall Street has assigned a "Strong Buy" consensus rating to GOOGL, META, NOW, ORCL, NVDA, and MSFT shares. Notably, Oracle offers the highest upside potential over the next twelve months.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: What Google Is Doing to Increase Its AI Chip Sales
data.description:
Tech giant Google (GOOGL) is looking to sell its artificial intelligence chips, called TPUs, to a wider range of customers, but doing so may be difficult. According to The Wall Street Journal, the main reason is that chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) already dominates the AI chip market, thereby making its hardware the default choice for many companies. And because so much software and infrastructure is built around Nvidia's technology, switching to a different platform can be complicated and risky.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Separately, it may also be hard to convince rival cloud providers such as Amazon Web Services (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure to use Google's chips, since they compete directly with Google's own cloud business. At the same time, supply could become limited. In fact, Google relies on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) to produce its TPUs, and TSM's biggest customer is Nvidia, which could cap available capacity.

Meanwhile, large potential buyers are already committing to Nvidia. For instance, Meta Platforms (META) recently signed a multiyear deal to purchase Nvidia chips. Nevertheless, Google is targeting smaller but growing customers instead of the largest cloud providers. Indeed, the company has approached so-called "neocloud" firms such as Fluidstack, which supplies computing power to AI startups. Google has also seen some adoption from AI developers themselves, including Anthropic, the creator of the Claude models, which already runs part of its workloads on TPUs.

Are Google Shares a Good Buy?

Turning to Wall Street, analysts have a Strong Buy consensus rating on GOOGL stock based on 26 Buys and seven Holds assigned in the past three months. Furthermore, the average GOOGL price target of $381.74 per share implies 21.2% upside potential.
data.author: Vince Condarcuri

data.title: Lucid (LCID) Lays Off 12% of U.S. Workforce ahead of Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Lucid Motors (LCID) is laying off 12% of its U.S. workforce, primarily in non-hourly and salaried roles, TechCrunch reported. This restructuring aims to improve gross margins and accelerate the luxury EV maker's path to profitability. The news comes just days before the company is slated to report its full-year 2025 results on February 24.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Hourly production workers at the Lucid manufacturing facility in Arizona, as well as logistics and quality teams, will not be impacted by this layoff. As of the end of 2024, the company employed about 6,800 full‑time workers globally.

The company said it will offer severance, bonuses, health coverage, and transition support to employees affected by the layoffs.

Strategic Reset in a Tough Market

Lucid has faced challenges since going public, including supply‑chain constraints, high production costs, and a premium price point that has limited its reach in a cooling EV market. The workforce reduction is part of a broader cost-cutting plan aimed at improving operational efficiency and extending the company's financial runway.

Despite the cuts, Lucid says it remains committed to its long‑term vision of building high‑performance EVs and expanding its lineup. That includes ramping production of its Gravity SUV and developing a more affordable EV priced around $48,000-$50,000, targeted for late 2026.

The company is betting that a leaner cost structure, combined with the launch of new models, will help it navigate the current downturn and position itself for future growth.

LCID to Report Q4 Earnings on February 24

Currently, analysts expect Lucid to post revenue of $459.53 million in Q4, up from $234.47 million in the year-ago quarter. Further, the company is expected to report a loss of $2.67 per share, compared with a loss of $2.20 in the prior-year quarter.

During the earnings call, analysts will be looking for updates on demand trends, cash burn, and Lucid's efforts to scale production in a cooling EV market.

Is Lucid a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Turning to Wall Street, LCID stock has a Moderate Sell consensus rating based on two Holds and one Sell assigned in the last three months. At $12.67, the average Lucid stock price target implies a 29.95% upside potential.
data.author: Radhika Saraogi

data.title: What Is Trump's Tariff Backup Plan?
data.description:
Following the Supreme Court's ruling against his tariffs, Trump labeled the decision a 'disgrace' and vowed to implement a backup plan.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

While Trump didn't elaborate further, he has several legal precedents to consider. One option is Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, which authorizes tariffs of up to 15% for a maximum of 150 days with possible extensions if approved by Congress. Trump could also invoke Section 338 of the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, which allows tariffs of up to 50% on countries deemed to engage in unfair trade practices with the U.S. Neither measure requires a federal investigation to be implemented.

Trump Has Several Tariff Backup Options

Two other options, which do require a federal investigation, are Section 201 and Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974. Tariffs under Section 201 require an investigation by the International Trade Commission with an initial duration of four years that can be extended by another four years. The maximum tariff rate is 50%, with a phase-down required after one year.

Tariffs under Section 301 require an investigation by the U.S. Trade Representative with an initial duration of four years and no maximum extension limit. Furthermore, this scenario carries no tariff rate limit.

data.author: Eddie Pan
